docs.title: Technical writer handbook
docs.text:
Welcome to the Technical Writer Handbook.

This handbook provides practical guidance for creating clear, consistent, and high-quality technical documentation.

It serves as a central reference for writing standards, documentation quality, and Docs-as-Code practices.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This handbook helps you:

  • Write clear and concise documentation.
  • Follow industry-standard writing practices.
  • Maintain consistency across documentation.
  • Improve documentation quality using automation.
  • Continuously improve your technical writing skills.

docs.title: Intended audience
docs.text:
This handbook is intended for:

  • Technical writers
  • Documentation leads
  • Product managers
  • Software engineers who contribute to documentation
  • Documentation reviewers

docs.title: Writing standard
docs.text:
This handbook adopts the Google Developer Documentation Style Guide as the primary writing standard.

As the handbook evolves, additional guidance and organization-specific standards may be added where appropriate.

docs.title: Handbook structure
docs.text:
The handbook currently includes the following sections:

  • Getting started
  • Writing standards
  • Documentation quality
  • Glossary

docs.title: Guiding principles
docs.text:
The handbook is based on the following principles:

  • Write for the reader.
  • Keep documentation clear and concise.
  • Be consistent.
  • Treat documentation as code.
  • Automate quality checks whenever possible.
  • Continuously improve documentation.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: If you're new to this handbook, begin with Getting started and then continue to Writing standards before exploring the remaining sections.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This glossary defines commonly used documentation and technical writing terms.

Term Definition Docs-as-Code Managing documentation using software development practices. Markdown A lightweight markup language used for writing documentation. MkDocs A static site generator for project documentation. Markdownlint A tool for validating Markdown formatting. Vale A prose linter for checking writing quality. Style guide A document that defines writing standards and conventions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Markdownlint is a tool that checks Markdown files for formatting issues and helps maintain a consistent documentation style.

It identifies common formatting problems and encourages writers to follow established Markdown best practices.

docs.title: Why use Markdownlint?
docs.text:
Markdownlint helps:

  • Improve documentation consistency.
  • Reduce formatting errors.
  • Improve readability.
  • Simplify document reviews.
  • Maintain a consistent writing style across the documentation.

docs.title: Current configuration
docs.text:
This handbook uses markdownlint-cli2 to validate Markdown files.

Markdownlint is configured using the .markdownlint.json file located in the project root.

docs.title: Common rules
docs.text:
The following Markdownlint rules are commonly encountered during documentation development.

Rule Description MD009 Trailing spaces MD010 Hard tabs MD013 Line length MD022 Blank lines around headings MD029 Ordered list numbering MD032 Blank lines around lists MD040 Language specified for fenced code blocks MD047 File ends with a single newline

docs.title: Running Markdownlint
docs.text:
Run Markdownlint for the entire project.

npx markdownlint-cli2 "docs/**/*.md"

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Follow these practices when writing Markdown:

  • Format documents consistently.
  • Fix linting issues before creating a pull request.
  • Use automatic formatting where possible.
  • Review lint warnings instead of ignoring them.
  • Keep Markdown simple and readable.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before submitting documentation, verify that:

  • Markdownlint completes without errors.
  • Markdown formatting is consistent.
  • Code blocks specify a language.
  • Documents end with a single newline.
  • Trailing spaces are removed.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
Imagine a documentation team with five technical writers working on the same documentation repository.

Each writer has a different writing and formatting style:

  • One writer uses tabs for indentation.
  • Another uses spaces.
  • One writer leaves trailing spaces.
  • Another skips blank lines between headings and content.
  • Some writers specify the language for code blocks, while others do not.

Although the documentation builds successfully, the Markdown files become inconsistent and difficult to maintain. Code reviews begin to focus on formatting issues instead of the quality of the content.

Markdownlint automatically identifies formatting issues and encourages every writer to follow the same Markdown conventions. As a result, documentation remains consistent, reviews become more efficient, and writers can focus on creating high-quality content instead of debating formatting preferences.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Markdownlint Documentation

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Vale reports alerts based on the configured writing standards. Understanding these alerts helps you improve the clarity, consistency, and readability of your documentation.

This page describes some of the most common alerts reported by the Google Developer Documentation Style Guide.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Identifies sentences written in the passive voice.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Instead of

Customer records are managed by the application.

Write

The application manages customer records.

docs.title: Best practice
docs.text: Prefer active voice when it improves clarity.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Checks whether headings follow sentence case.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Instead of

Configure Billing Rules

Write

Configure billing rules

docs.title: Best practice
docs.text: Use sentence case for all headings.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Identifies acronyms that may be unfamiliar to readers.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Instead of

Configure the LDC.

Write

Configure the Local Distribution Company (LDC).

docs.title: Best practice
docs.text: Define unfamiliar acronyms on first use.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Identifies unnecessary use of parentheses.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Instead of

Configure billing rules (if applicable).

Write

If applicable, configure billing rules.

docs.title: Best practice
docs.text: Use parentheses only when they improve clarity.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Checks for the use of the Oxford comma in lists.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Instead of

Billing, reporting and analytics

Write

Billing, reporting, and analytics

docs.title: Best practice
docs.text: Use the Oxford comma in lists.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Checks for spaces before and after an em dash.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Instead of

Billing — Reporting

Write

Billing—Reporting

docs.title: Best practice
docs.text: Use em dashes consistently.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
When reviewing Vale alerts:

  • Read the alert message carefully.
  • Review the surrounding context.
  • Apply the recommendation only if it improves the writing.
  • Do not accept suggestions automatically.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

  • Common Vale alerts have been reviewed.
  • Changes improve readability.
  • Documentation follows the approved writing standard.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Vale Documentation
  • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
After installing Vale, configure it to use the writing standards for your documentation project.

Vale reads its configuration from a .vale.ini file located in the project root.

docs.title: Create the configuration file
docs.text:
Create a file named .vale.ini in the root directory of your project.

Example:

project/
├── docs/
├── styles/
├── .vale.ini
└── mkdocs.yml

docs.title: Basic configuration
docs.text:
The following configuration uses the Google Developer Documentation Style Guide.

StylesPath = styles

MinAlertLevel = suggestion

Packages = Google

[*.md]
BasedOnStyles = Google

docs.title: Configuration options
docs.text: Option Description StylesPath Directory that contains Vale styles. MinAlertLevel Minimum alert level to report. Packages Style packages to install. BasedOnStyles Writing styles to apply to Markdown files.

docs.title: Synchronize style packages
docs.text:
After saving the configuration file, download the configured style packages.

vale sync

docs.title: Verify the configuration
docs.text:
Run the following command to verify that Vale can locate the configuration file.

vale ls-config

The command displays the active configuration used by Vale.

docs.title: Validate documentation
docs.text:
Run Vale against a Markdown file.

vale docs/index.md

Run Vale against all Markdown files.

vale docs

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
• Store .vale.ini in the project root.
  • Commit .vale.ini to source control.
  • Keep the configuration consistent across the team.
  • Review changes to the configuration during code reviews.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before continuing, verify that:

  • .vale.ini exists in the project root.
  • The Google style package is installed.
  • vale sync completes successfully.
  • vale ls-config displays the active configuration.
  • Vale analyzes your Markdown files successfully.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Continue to Understanding Vale alerts to learn how to interpret and resolve Vale suggestions, warnings, and errors.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Vale Documentation
  • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Vale includes built-in style packages, such as the Google Developer Documentation Style Guide. However, every documentation team has its own terminology, writing conventions, and documentation standards.

Custom rules allow you to extend existing style guides and enforce organization-specific writing standards.

docs.title: When to create custom rules
docs.text:
Consider creating custom rules when:

  • Your organization uses product-specific terminology.
  • You want to enforce internal writing standards.
  • Existing Vale rules do not cover your requirements.
  • Writers frequently make the same writing mistakes.
  • You want to improve consistency across the documentation.

docs.title: Common use cases
docs.text:
Custom rules can help enforce:

  • Product terminology
  • Approved acronyms
  • UI writing conventions
  • Preferred word choices
  • Prohibited words or phrases
  • Company-specific writing standards

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
A documentation team decides to always use End user instead of End User.

A custom Vale rule can identify incorrect usage and recommend the approved term.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
When creating custom rules:

  • Start with an existing style guide before creating custom rules.
  • Create custom rules only when there is a clear business need.
  • Keep rules simple and easy to understand.
  • Document the purpose of every custom rule.
  • Review custom rules regularly as writing standards evolve.

docs.title: Rule organization
docs.text:
Store custom rules in a dedicated style package.

styles/
├── Google/
└── TW/
    ├── Terminology.yml
    ├── UIWriting.yml
    ├── ProductNames.yml
    └── Vocabulary/

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before creating a custom rule, verify that:

  • The rule is not already covered by the existing style guide.
  • The writing standard has been documented.
  • The rule improves documentation consistency.
  • The rule benefits all contributors.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Vale Documentation
  • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Vale is available for Windows, macOS, and Linux. Install Vale on your development machine before using it to review documentation.

docs.title: Install Vale
docs.text:
=== "macOS"

Install Vale using Homebrew.

```bash
brew install vale
```

=== "Windows"

Install Vale using WinGet.

```powershell
winget install errata-ai.Vale
```

=== "Linux"

Follow the installation instructions for your Linux distribution in the Vale documentation.

docs.title: Verify the installation
docs.text:
Run the following command to verify that Vale is installed.

vale --version

Example output:

vale version 3.15.1

docs.title: Update Vale
docs.text:
Keep Vale up to date to receive the latest features, bug fixes, and writing rules.

=== "macOS"

```bash
brew upgrade vale
```

=== "Windows"

```powershell
winget upgrade errata-ai.Vale
```

docs.title: Uninstall Vale
docs.text:
=== "macOS"

```bash
brew uninstall vale
```

=== "Windows"

```powershell
winget uninstall errata-ai.Vale
```

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before continuing, verify that:

  • Vale is installed.
  • The vale --version command runs successfully.
  • Vale is available from the terminal.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Continue to Configuring Vale to configure Vale for your documentation project.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Vale Documentation

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Vale analyzes your documentation and reports issues based on the configured writing standards.

Each alert includes information that helps you identify the issue, locate it in the document, and determine whether it should be addressed.

docs.title: Alert format
docs.text:
A typical Vale alert looks like this:

docs/getting-started/index.md
7:167 suggestion Use active voice instead of passive voice.
Google.Passive

docs.title: Understanding the alert
docs.text: Component Description docs/getting-started/index.md File containing the issue. 7:167 Line number and column number. suggestion Alert severity. Use active voice instead of passive voice. Description of the issue. Google.Passive Writing rule that triggered the alert.

docs.title: Alert severity
docs.text:
Vale reports the following alert levels:

Severity Description Suggestion A recommendation to improve the writing. Warning A potential issue that should be reviewed. Error A rule violation that should be corrected.

docs.title: Should every alert be fixed?
docs.text:
No.

Vale provides recommendations based on the configured writing standards. Review each alert before making changes.

Consider the following questions:

  • Does the suggestion improve clarity?
  • Does the suggestion preserve the intended meaning?
  • Is the alert applicable to the current context?
  • Does the documentation follow the team's writing standard?

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
When reviewing Vale alerts:

  • Read the complete alert message.
  • Understand why the rule was triggered.
  • Review the surrounding content before making changes.
  • Use judgment instead of accepting every suggestion automatically.
  • Keep the reader's experience as the highest priority.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before submitting documentation, verify that:

  • All Vale errors have been resolved.
  • Warnings have been reviewed.
  • Suggestions have been evaluated.
  • Changes improve the readability of the documentation.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text: Continue to Common Google style rules to learn about frequently reported Vale alerts and how to address them.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Vale Documentation
  • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Vale is a prose linter that helps technical writers improve the quality, consistency, and readability of documentation.

Unlike Markdownlint, which checks Markdown formatting, Vale analyzes the writing itself. It identifies issues related to grammar, style, capitalization, terminology, readability, and other writing conventions.

docs.title: Why use Vale?
docs.text:
Vale helps technical writers:

  • Improve writing quality.
  • Maintain a consistent writing style.
  • Detect common writing issues early.
  • Reduce review comments.
  • Focus code reviews on content instead of writing style.

docs.title: What does Vale check?
docs.text:
Depending on the configured style guide, Vale can identify issues such as:

  • Passive voice
  • Inconsistent capitalization
  • Incorrect heading style
  • Unnecessary parentheses
  • Acronyms
  • Repeated words
  • Word choice
  • Punctuation
  • Terminology

docs.title: Writing standard
docs.text:
This handbook uses the Google Developer Documentation Style Guide as the primary writing standard.

Vale validates documentation against the configured writing standards and reports suggestions, warnings, and errors.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
When using Vale:

  • Run Vale before submitting a pull request.
  • Review every suggestion before making changes.
  • Do not accept every suggestion automatically.
  • Use judgment when applying writing recommendations.
  • Follow the approved writing standard consistently.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before submitting documentation, verify that:

  • Vale completes without errors.
  • Suggestions have been reviewed.
  • Warnings have been addressed where appropriate.
  • The documentation follows the approved writing standard.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Vale Documentation
  • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Welcome to the Technical Writer Handbook.

This handbook provides practical guidance for writing and maintaining technical documentation using industry best practices.

It is designed to be used as a day-to-day reference rather than a book.

docs.title: Before you begin
docs.text:
Familiarize yourself with:

  • Markdown
  • Git
  • MkDocs
  • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Guiding principles
docs.text:
• Write for the reader.
  • Keep documentation simple.
  • Be consistent.
  • Prefer clarity over cleverness.
  • Continuously improve documentation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Writing standards ensure that documentation remains clear, consistent, and easy to understand regardless of who writes it.

This handbook adopts the Google Developer Documentation Style Guide as the primary writing standard.

Company-specific writing standards will supplement Google's guidance when necessary.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
Focus on:

  • Voice and tone
  • Formatting
  • Consistency
  • Readability
  • User-focused writing

Refer to the following sections for detailed guidance.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Consistent formatting improves readability and makes documentation easier to scan, review, and maintain.

This section provides general formatting guidelines for creating clear and consistent technical documentation.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these formatting guidelines:

  • Use sentence case for headings.
  • Use numbered lists for procedures.
  • Use bullet lists for unordered information.
  • Format UI elements using backticks.
  • Format file names and commands using backticks.
  • Use fenced code blocks for code samples.
  • Keep paragraphs short and focused.
  • Use tables only when they improve readability.

docs.title: Headings
docs.text:
Use sentence case for all headings.

Instead of

Configure Billing Rules

Write

Configure billing rules

docs.title: Lists
docs.text:
Use numbered lists for sequential tasks.

1. Create an LDC.
2. Configure the billing rules.
3. Save the configuration.

Use bullet lists for related information.

- Customer details
- Billing information
- Contact information

docs.title: UI elements
docs.text:
Format UI elements using backticks.

Instead of

Click Save.

Write

Click Save.

docs.title: File names
docs.text:
Format file names using backticks.

Examples:

  • mkdocs.yml
  • README.md
  • package.json

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
Use fenced code blocks for commands.

mkdocs serve

docs.title: Code
docs.text:
Use fenced code blocks with the appropriate language.

site_name: Technical Writer Handbook
theme:
  name: material

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

Headings use sentence case. Lists are formatted consistently. UI elements use backticks. File names use backticks. Commands use fenced code blocks. Paragraphs are concise. Tables improve readability.

docs.title: References
docs.text: Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Headings organize content and help readers quickly find the information they need. Use clear, descriptive headings to improve readability and navigation.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these guidelines when writing headings:

  • Use sentence case.
  • Keep headings concise and descriptive.
  • Organize headings in a logical hierarchy.
  • Avoid skipping heading levels.
  • Use headings that describe the content that follows.
  • Avoid vague or generic headings.

docs.title: Use sentence case
docs.text:
Capitalize only the first word and proper nouns.

Instead of

Configure Billing Rules

Write

Configure billing rules

docs.title: Write descriptive headings
docs.text:
Choose headings that clearly describe the content.

Instead of

Configuration

Write

Configure billing rules

docs.title: Keep headings concise
docs.text:
Keep headings short and easy to scan.

Instead of

Steps to configure the billing rules for an LDC

Write

Configure billing rules

docs.title: Use a logical hierarchy
docs.text:
Organize headings in a consistent structure.

# Configure billing rules

## Create a billing rule

## Edit a billing rule

## Delete a billing rule

docs.title: Avoid skipping heading levels
docs.text:
Maintain a logical heading hierarchy.

Instead of

# Configure billing rules

### Create a billing rule

Write

# Configure billing rules

## Create a billing rule

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

  • Headings use sentence case.
  • Headings clearly describe the content.
  • Headings are concise.
  • Heading levels follow a logical hierarchy.
  • Heading levels are not skipped.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Images should support the content by helping readers understand a concept or complete a task. Do not use images as a substitute for written instructions.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these guidelines when using images:

  • Use images only when they add value.
  • Keep screenshots clear and readable.
  • Crop images to highlight relevant content.
  • Keep annotations simple.
  • Update images when the UI changes.

docs.title: Use screenshots when necessary
docs.text: Use screenshots to highlight complex user interface elements.

docs.title: Keep images focused
docs.text: Show only the relevant portion of the screen.

docs.title: Use annotations sparingly
docs.text: Use arrows or highlights only when they improve clarity.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

  • Images are clear.
  • Images support the content.
  • Images are current.
  • Images are appropriately cropped.
  • Annotations are easy to understand.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Links help readers navigate related information. Use descriptive link text that clearly communicates the destination.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these guidelines when adding links:

  • Use meaningful link text.
  • Avoid "click here" or "read more."
  • Link only when it adds value.
  • Verify that links are valid.

docs.title: Use descriptive links
docs.text:
Instead of

Click here.

Write

For more information, see Configure billing rules.

docs.title: Avoid unnecessary links
docs.text: Only add links that help the reader complete a task or understand a concept.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

  • Link text is descriptive.
  • Links are relevant.
  • Links are valid.
  • Broken links are removed.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Lists make documentation easier to read and scan. Use lists to present related information or sequential tasks.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these guidelines when writing lists:

  • Use numbered lists for sequential tasks.
  • Use bullet lists for related information.
  • Keep list items concise.
  • Write list items in a parallel structure.
  • Keep punctuation consistent.

docs.title: Use numbered lists for procedures
docs.text:
1. Open the application.
2. Select `Administration`.
3. Click `Save`.

docs.title: Use bullet lists for related information
docs.text:
- Customer information
- Billing information
- Contact information

docs.title: Use parallel structure
docs.text:
Instead of

- Configure billing rules
- Customer management
- Creating reports

Write

- Configure billing rules
- Manage customers
- Generate reports

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

  • The correct list type is used.
  • List items are concise.
  • List items follow a parallel structure.
  • Punctuation is consistent.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Procedures help users complete a task by providing clear, step-by-step instructions.

Well-written procedures are easy to follow, concise, and action-oriented.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these guidelines when writing procedures:

  • Use numbered lists for sequential steps.
  • Begin each step with an action verb.
  • Describe one action per step.
  • Write in the imperative mood.
  • Present steps in the order they are performed.
  • Include additional information only when it helps the reader complete the task.

docs.title: Use numbered lists
docs.text:
Use numbered lists for all sequential procedures.

Example

  1. Open the application.
  2. Select Administration.
  3. Click Users.
  4. Click Add User.

docs.title: Start each step with an action
docs.text:
Begin each step with an action verb.

Instead of

The user should click Save.

Write

Click Save.

docs.title: Describe one action per step
docs.text:
Each step should describe a single action.

Instead of

Open the application, configure the settings, and save the changes.

Write

  1. Open the application.
  2. Configure the settings.
  3. Click Save.

docs.title: Keep steps concise
docs.text:
Keep instructions short and easy to follow.

Instead of

Navigate to the Administration page where you can configure user settings for the application.

Write

Go to Administration.

docs.title: Provide context when necessary
docs.text:
Add a note only when additional information helps the reader complete the task.

Note

Administrator privileges are required to perform this procedure.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing a procedure, verify that:

  • Steps are presented in the correct order.
  • Each step begins with an action verb.
  • Each step describes one action.
  • Procedures use numbered lists.
  • UI elements are formatted using backticks.
  • Additional information is included only when necessary.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Consistent terminology improves clarity and helps readers understand the documentation. Use approved terms consistently throughout the documentation.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these guidelines when using terminology:

  • Use consistent terminology.
  • Use product terminology consistently.
  • Define unfamiliar acronyms on first use.
  • Avoid using multiple terms for the same concept.
  • Use industry-standard terminology whenever possible.

docs.title: Be consistent
docs.text:
Instead of

Customer
Client
Account

Use a single approved term throughout the documentation.

docs.title: Define acronyms
docs.text:
Local Distribution Company (LDC)

Use LDC in subsequent references.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

  • Terminology is consistent.
  • Acronyms are defined on first use.
  • Product terminology matches the application.
  • Synonyms are avoided.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Use consistent terminology when referring to user interface (UI) elements. Clearly distinguish UI labels from regular text.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these guidelines when writing about UI elements:

  • Format UI elements using backticks.
  • Use the exact UI label.
  • Use sentence case unless the UI uses a different style.
  • Use the imperative mood for instructions.
  • Avoid unnecessary words.

docs.title: Buttons
docs.text:
Instead of

Click Save.

Write

Click Save.

docs.title: Menu paths
docs.text: Go to `Administration > Users`.

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Enter the customer name in `Customer Name`.

docs.title: Check boxes
docs.text: Select the `Active` checkbox.

docs.title: Dialog boxes
docs.text: Click `OK`.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

  • UI labels use backticks.
  • UI labels match the application.
  • Instructions use the imperative mood.
  • Menu paths are accurate.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The voice and tone of your documentation determine how readers experience your content. Effective technical documentation is clear, direct, professional, and focused on helping users accomplish their goals.

This handbook adopts the Google Developer Documentation Style Guide as the primary writing standard.

docs.title: Guidelines
docs.text:
Follow these guidelines when writing documentation:

  • Prefer active voice.
  • Write in the present tense.
  • Use the imperative mood for procedures.
  • Write for the reader.
  • Keep sentences concise.
  • Use simple and familiar language.
  • Avoid unnecessary jargon.
  • Be consistent in terminology and style.

docs.title: Use active voice
docs.text:
Prefer active voice whenever it improves clarity.

Instead of

Customer records are managed by the application.

Write

The application manages customer records.

docs.title: Use the present tense
docs.text:
Describe how the product works today.

Instead of

The application will display the report.

Write

The application displays the report.

docs.title: Use the imperative mood
docs.text:
Write procedural steps as direct instructions.

Instead of

The Save button should be clicked.

Write

Click Save.

docs.title: Write for the reader
docs.text:
Focus on what the reader wants to accomplish.

Instead of

The Billing page provides functionality for configuring billing rules.

Write

Configure billing rules on the Billing page.

docs.title: Keep sentences concise
docs.text: Express one idea per sentence whenever possible.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Avoid Preferred The Save button should be clicked. Click Save. The application will generate the report. The application generates the report. Customer records are managed by the application. The application manages customer records. Click on Save. Click Save.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
Before publishing documentation, verify that:

  • Active voice is used where appropriate.
  • The present tense is used consistently.
  • Procedures use the imperative mood.
  • Sentences are concise and easy to read.
  • Terminology is consistent throughout the document.

docs.title: References
docs.text: • Google Developer Documentation Style Guide
